did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning (specifically the ResNet50 architecture) and image enhancement techniques to perform skin cancer detection, segmentation, and classification tasks. The study achieved 98.7% segmentation accuracy and 98.8% classification accuracy on the ISIC 2018 dataset using these AI methods.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, specifically a novel approach using image enhancement and ResNet50 deep learning models for skin cancer detection. It reports specific performance metrics (98.7% segmentation accuracy and 98.8% classification accuracy) on the ISIC 2018 dataset, describes a proposed system with segmentation and classification phases, and details the methodology and results of their own study rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Novel Approach Using Image Enhancement and AI Techniques for Enhancing Skin Cancer Detection with Deep Learning
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Computing Research On Science Engineering and Technology (ACROSET)
brief_summary: This study proposes an Automated Skin Cancer Detection (ASCD) system integrating image enhancement techniques with the ResNet50 deep learning architecture to improve melanoma diagnosis. Utilizing the ISIC 2018 dataset, the methodology involves a tailored segmentation phase to address low-contrast images and noise, followed by a classification phase using optimized feature subsets. The model was trained on 80% of the data and tested on the remaining 20%. The system achieved a segmentation accuracy of 98.7% and a classification accuracy of 98.8%. These results demonstrate that combining state-of-the-art deep learning with preprocessing methods significantly enhances diagnostic precision and efficiency for skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as the most aggressive and fatal kind of skin cancer in the introduction. Specifically mentioned as a class within the ISIC 2018 dataset used for training and validation. Fig. 3 shows segmentation of a melanoma image.
Listed as one of the seven categories in the ISIC 2018 skin lesion dataset used in the study.

all_task_types.details: The paper implements a tailored image segmentation phase using edge-based detection and morphological operations to isolate skin lesions from the background. It reports a segmentation accuracy of 98.7% on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign/Malignant)
all_task_types.details: The primary task is skin cancer detection/classification using a ResNet50 model. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (seven categories including Melanoma, Nevus, and Basal Cell Carcinoma) and reports a classification accuracy of 98.8% on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopy dataset widely recognized for skin cancer research, containing images with high variability in lesion type, size, and appearance, along with metadata such as patient demographics and lesion locations. It includes ground truth annotations for segmentation masks and diagnosis labels.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet50 model (80% of the data) and for the segmentation phase.
use.testing: Used for testing the ResNet50 model (20% of the data) and evaluating segmentation and classification performance.
use.other: Used to address data skewness via weighted cross-entropy and to validate the model's robustness.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy metrics (98.7% segmentation accuracy and 98.8% classification accuracy) and compared qualitatively to other methods.

segmentation_phase.notes: The proposed method achieved 98.7% segmentation accuracy, outperforming Active Contours [10] and Superpixel-Based Masked Image Modeling [11]. The segmentation phase utilized morphological operations and histogram-based contrast enhancement.

classification_phase.notes: The ResNet50 model achieved 98.8% classification accuracy on the ISIC 2018 dataset, outperforming Optimized CNN [12] and Bootstrapping Model [13]. The model was trained on 80% of the data and tested on the remaining 20%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported, but described as a 'pre-trained' network in the context of transfer learning capabilities and residual connections minimizing vanishing gradients.

key_components: Residual connections to minimize vanishing gradients
Bottleneck architecture using 1x1 convolutions to reduce dimensionality
architecture_details.feature_processing: Optimized feature subsets generated via feature weight assignment and subset generation (correlation and dependency analysis) were fed into the model.

training_details.optimization: Not explicitly reported (Adam and RMSprop mentioned for other models in comparison table, but not confirmed for ResNet50 in this study)

classification_or_output_head.classes: Seven categories (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign, Malignant, etc.)

preprocessing_techniques: Artifact removal (hair, air bubbles, shadows, pen marks) using morphological operations and painting techniques
Morphological operations (dilation and erosion) for noise reduction and edge definition enhancement
Histogram-based contrast enhancement (splitting histogram into sub-histograms and independent equalization)
Image normalization to standardize pixel intensity values
Edge-based segmentation to isolate lesions from background
Data augmentation (flipping, rotating, and zooming images)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as a data augmentation method during training to make the model more resistant to changes in input data.
Used as a data augmentation method during training to make the model more resistant to changes in input data.

conclusions: The integration of ResNet50 deep learning with tailored image enhancement techniques (morphological operations and histogram-based contrast enhancement) significantly improves the precision and reliability of automated skin cancer detection.

The proposed segmentation phase achieved a high accuracy of 98.7% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, outperforming traditional methods such as Active Contours (82.6%) and Superpixel-Based Masked Image Modeling (96.21%).

The Skin Cancer Classification Phase model utilizing ResNet50 achieved a classification accuracy of 98.8% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, surpassing existing methods like the Optimized CNN (82%) and Bootstrapping Model (93.8%).
The use of weighted feature subsets reduced computational complexity while maintaining high classification accuracy, making the system suitable for low-resource or mobile environments.
The system addresses challenges associated with low-contrast images and noise (such as hair and shadows), facilitating clearer lesion boundaries and improved diagnostic outcomes.
The lightweight nature of the optimized model allows for fast inference, supporting potential applications in remote diagnostics and tele-dermatology.

Future work should focus on clinical tests, studies with healthcare professionals to assess usability and explainability (using tools like SHAP and Grad-CAM), and deployment into regular clinical workflows.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset for both training and evaluation, lacking external validation on independent datasets which limits generalizability assessment.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance claims are based solely on accuracy metrics (98.7% segmentation, 98.8% classification).

The paper does not explicitly report the pretraining weights or source for the ResNet50 model, nor does it detail the specific optimizer used for the proposed ResNet50 model (only mentioning Adam/RMSprop for comparative models).
The dataset split is reported as a simple 80/20 train-test split without mention of cross-validation or stratification, raising potential concerns about data leakage or split bias.

The paper acknowledges that DL models may be biased or struggle to generalize to other populations due to reliance on large, high-quality datasets, but does not provide demographic analysis of the ISIC 2018 subset used.

The system's clinical applicability is currently theoretical; the authors explicitly state that future work must include clinical tests and studies with healthcare professionals to assess usability and explainability.

While the paper mentions using SHAP for explainability in the implementation section, it does not present any SHAP or Grad-CAM visualizations or results in the provided text, limiting the assessment of model interpretability.

The comparison with prior works (e.g., Optimized CNN, Bootstrapping Model) is limited to accuracy metrics without providing details on whether those comparative models were re-implemented under identical conditions or if they are cited from literature.
